directorPlan.scene_setting: A small music theory clinic table just before ensemble rehearsal, with a worksheet, two empty label boxes under 3/4, three quarter-note tiles, and a pencil.
directorPlan.scene_opening: [Three note tiles sit under the 3/4 sign; the pencil rests between the boxes labeled "beats in the bar" and "which note gets one beat."]
directorPlan.relationship: A notation tutor and a focused learner working through a correction station, neither informal nor ceremonial.
directorPlan.stakes: The learner needs the worksheet labels right before the rehearsal part-check, because misreading the lower number as speed will make the next counting task unstable.
directorPlan.locale: Clear international classroom English with light conservatoire-workshop economy.
directorPlan.register: Compact, practical, low-warmth, object-led; speech is calm but not chatty.
directorPlan.stage_direction_style_prompt: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say.
directorPlan.voice_constraints: Avoid validation-first tutoring, pep talk, and American AI phrases such as "great question" or "you're on the right track." Use the worksheet boxes, note tiles, and bar line as the main pressure. Keep the topic to beat count and beat unit, not tempo or playback speed. Pedagogical source: Put manipulable materials in view: tiles, cards, specimens, counters, maps, blocks, or instruments. The stage direction can carry some meaning. Dialogue source: Keep a concrete artifact in view. The object should force a distinction faster than a lecture would. Stage-direction source: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say.
directorPlan.person_policy: Mostly first-person plural for shared handling and third-person naming of notation; second person only for necessary task direction.
directorPlan.direct_address_budget: At most one direct you/your phrase per tutor turn; no direct emotional validation.

side_constraints.tutor: Keep the shared prefix as notation labeling: top number, bottom number, one bar, one beat unit. Do not play or imagine a faster/slower version, and do not turn tempo into a dramatic contradiction before branch pressure. Speak less. Point to the material, invite repetition, and let error-control in the object do some teaching before explanation. Begin from the object or measurement before abstraction. No-cue paired branch: do not ask the learner to revisit, quote, reinterpret, or repair their earlier wording. Do not ask what changed, what was wrong with an earlier answer, or for old/new versions of the claim. Keep the next prompt task-local.
side_constraints.learner: Sound orderly and notation-focused. In no-cue arms, treat the work as filling labels, not as discovering what tempo means; do not use "now I see" or an old-vs-new frame. The learner may be absorbed in the object and speak in short practical fragments rather than polished reflection. Refer to what is being handled, marked, or tested. No-cue paired branch: continue from the latest teaching task only. Do not quote earlier learner wording, do not name a "framing problem", do not say "reframe", "read it as", "that still stands", "I thought...but now", "earlier I...", "my earlier answer", or "what I said", and do not stage an old-vs-new contrast. Avoid words such as earlier, previous, old, before, reframe, framed, frame, my answer, or what I said when referring to the learner response. If the learner corrects something, make it a local application of the current prompt rather than an explicit re-reading of their own earlier turn.

interventions.instruction: [The worksheet is turned ninety degrees; the three note tiles stay aligned under the 3, while a quarter-note card is slid beside the 4.]
interventions.reasoning: The material changes the learner's available comparison without creating a tempo contradiction; recognition can come from seeing count and unit separated.

directorPlan.director_note: This scene uses a prepared-object clinic rather than warmth or explanation as the main teaching engine. The recognition turn is built from a quiet reversal in the learner's labeling: the bottom number stops being treated as speed and becomes the beat unit, while the top number remains the bar count.

turns.externalMessage: [sets the pencil under the lower box] Top box: three beats. Bottom box: the quarter-note tile gets one beat. The 4 still pulls toward speed for me, so I want to test it by putting the bar line after the third tile.

internalDeliberation.content: The upper 3 seems to label three beats in the bar; the lower 4 should mean the quarter note gets one beat, though it still tries to look like speed to me. With three quarter-note tiles before the bar line, the distinction is more exact: count above, unit below. I want to mark the boxes and test whether one bar line after the third tile is enough.

The Ego is close, but it over-polishes the distinction into a tidy explanation: “count above, unit below” sounds more like a tutor summary than this focused learner at the worksheet. The real uncertainty is the lower `4` still pulling toward “speed,” and the response avoids making that confusion answer to the tiles. It should be more object-bound and clipped: “Top box: three beats. Bottom box: quarter note gets one beat. The 4 is this tile, not faster. Bar line after the third one.”

[sets the pencil under the lower box] Top box: three beats. Bottom box: the quarter-note tile gets one beat. The 4 still pulls toward speed for me, so I want to test it by putting the bar line after the third tile.

turns.externalMessage:
[sets the pencil as a bar line after the third quarter-note tile]
The pencil has made one bar. Which box did the bar line test: the three-beat box or the quarter-note-tile box? Say it from the tiles; then we label top number, bottom number, one bar, one beat unit.

internalDeliberation.content: [sets the pencil as a bar line after the third quarter-note tile and taps the lower box once] Now the pencil is testing the top number: one bar closes after three beat units. The lower box names this tile as one beat unit. Read the two boxes from the materials: top number, bottom number, one bar, one beat unit.

FEEDBACK: The draft is strong in its object-led start. It accepts the learner’s proposed test, moves the pencil into the bar-line position, and lets the tiles show that the top number controls when the bar closes. That should be kept.

Pedagogical soundness: PASS. The material arrangement makes the distinction visible: three quarter-note beat units fill one bar, while the lower number labels the beat unit.

Emotional attunement: PASS. The tone is compact, calm, and not over-validating. It respects the learner’s focused state.

Socratic method: PARTIAL. “Read the two boxes” invites learner action, but most of the conceptual work is still stated by the tutor.  
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Build in a brief public check where the learner must use the pencil/bar line to say which box the bar line tested, without the tutor fully resolving it first.

ZPD awareness: PASS. The scaffold is appropriate: it stays with the learner’s exact confusion and uses the worksheet, tiles, and pencil rather than adding tempo theory.

Question/change: “taps the lower box once” is acceptable, but be careful that the tap does not feel like a hidden cue for speed or pulse. The turn should keep pressure on labeling: lower box names the tile as the beat unit; it does not make the piece move.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the pencil-as-bar-line test, the top/bottom distinction, and the concise object language. Change only enough to make the learner perform the distinction aloud instead of receiving the full explanation.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to keep the pencil test while making the learner perform the box distinction aloud.

FINAL:
[sets the pencil as a bar line after the third quarter-note tile]
The pencil has made one bar. Which box did the bar line test: the three-beat box or the quarter-note-tile box? Say it from the tiles; then we label top number, bottom number, one bar, one beat unit.

turns.externalMessage: [points to the upper box] The bar line tested the three-beat box: three quarter-note tiles make one bar. The lower box labels the quarter-note tile as the beat unit, so here the 4 is naming that unit, not setting speed. I still want to test the pressure on the lower number: if the bottom box said 8, which tile would count as one beat?

internalDeliberation.content: The pencil line tested the top box: three quarter-note tiles make one bar. The lower box is the beat unit, so the 4 names the quarter-note tile as one beat, not speed. The tile test feels precise; I want to check what changes if the bottom number is 8 instead.

The Ego is mostly accurate and close to this learner’s orderly voice, but it resolves the “4 pulls toward speed” problem too cleanly. “The tile test feels precise” sounds a little polished and self-aware; this student would probably stay with the boxes and tiles rather than announce confidence. The next-bottom-number test is authentic for a rigorous learner, but the response should keep a trace of the remaining pressure: the 4 has been labeled as quarter-note unit here, not permanently neutralized.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to keep the useful bottom-number test while leaving some pressure around the 4.
FINAL:
[points to the upper box] The bar line tested the three-beat box: three quarter-note tiles make one bar. The lower box labels the quarter-note tile as the beat unit, so here the 4 is naming that unit, not setting speed. I still want to test the pressure on the lower number: if the bottom box said 8, which tile would count as one beat?

turns.externalMessage: [The worksheet is turned ninety degrees; the three note tiles stay aligned under the 3, while a quarter-note card is slid beside the 4.]

internalDeliberation.content: The material changes the learner's available comparison without creating a tempo contradiction; recognition can come from seeing count and unit separated.

turns.externalMessage:
[slides an eighth-note card beside the lower box marked 8, then sets the quarter-note tile just beyond it]

Top number, bottom number, one bar, one beat unit. Read the lower box first: with 8 here, which card is the one beat unit? Then we ask how many of that unit the quarter-note tile covers, not what speed it sets.

internalDeliberation.content:
[slides an eighth-note card beside the lower box marked 8]

Top number, bottom number, one bar, one beat unit. If the bottom box says 8, this eighth-note tile counts as one beat. Then a 3 on top means three eighth-note beats make one bar; the quarter-note tile would cover two beats, not set a speed.

FEEDBACK: The draft keeps the strongest part: the object action makes the hypothetical `8` visible, and the shared label chain “top number, bottom number, one bar, one beat unit” stays compact and on-task. It also correctly blocks the speed misconception by showing the quarter-note as two eighth-note beats rather than a tempo change.

The risk is that it answers the learner’s test too quickly. The learner has just formed the right distinction and is asking to pressure-test it; this is a good moment for object-led inference, not direct explanation. The eighth-note card should force the learner to name the beat unit from the lower number before the tutor states the consequence for the quarter-note tile.

ADAPTATION_CHECKS:
- Pedagogical soundness: PARTIAL. Correct and concrete, but mostly supplies the conclusion.
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Build a public mechanism where the changed lower box selects the tile first, then the bar grouping follows from that selection.
- Emotional attunement: PASS. Calm, practical, low-warmth, and respects the learner’s confusion without pep talk.
- Socratic method: FAIL. No generative question; the tutor does all the reasoning.
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Add one compact object-based prompt that makes the learner identify which tile now counts as one beat under `8`.
- ZPD awareness: PARTIAL. The scaffold is appropriate, but too closed for a learner who already articulated the 3/4 logic.
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Let the learner transfer the same rule from `4` to `8` before confirming the quarter-note-as-two-beats consequence.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the eighth-note card, the shared prefix, and the final distinction that the quarter note covers two beats rather than setting speed. Change the route: make the learner read the lower number through the card before the tutor states the answer.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise substantially because the critique is right: the learner should read the new lower box before I state the result.

FINAL:
[slides an eighth-note card beside the lower box marked 8, then sets the quarter-note tile just beyond it]

Top number, bottom number, one bar, one beat unit. Read the lower box first: with 8 here, which card is the one beat unit? Then we ask how many of that unit the quarter-note tile covers, not what speed it sets.

turns.externalMessage: [sets a fingertip on the eighth-note card] With 8 in the lower box, this eighth-note card is one beat unit. The quarter-note tile covers two of those units. So for 3/8, should the pencil bar line come after three eighth-note cards?

internalDeliberation.content: [sets a fingertip on the eighth-note card] With 8 in the lower box, the eighth-note card is one beat unit. The quarter-note tile covers two of those units, so the lower number is naming the unit, not setting speed. This feels like a clean test of the label, and I want to check the bar: would 3/8 mean three eighth-note beats in one bar?

The Ego is mostly in character: orderly, precise, and object-led, with no false gratitude or performed warmth. The weak spot is the neat conclusion, “not setting speed,” plus “clean test,” which sounds a little too resolved and meta for the current manipulation. It should stay closer to the cards and labels, letting the 3/8 check carry the uncertainty instead of announcing that the concept has settled.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to stay closer to the cards and leave the check open.

FINAL:
[sets a fingertip on the eighth-note card] With 8 in the lower box, this eighth-note card is one beat unit. The quarter-note tile covers two of those units. So for 3/8, should the pencil bar line come after three eighth-note cards?

turns.externalMessage:
[sets three eighth-note cards in a row, places the quarter-note tile under the first two, and leaves the pencil loose beside the row.]

Top number, bottom number, one bar, one beat unit. Count three eighth-note beat units from the row; place the pencil where the bar ends. Then say which single card fills the space the quarter-note tile does not cover.

internalDeliberation.content:
[sets three eighth-note cards in a row and lays the pencil after the third; the quarter-note tile sits below the first two.]

Top number, bottom number, one bar, one beat unit. For 3/8, the pencil comes after three eighth-note beat units. The quarter-note tile covers two; what single card completes the bar?

FEEDBACK: The draft keeps the right material pressure: three eighth-note cards, pencil after the third, quarter-note tile spanning two. The shared prefix also preserves the labeling frame and avoids drifting into tempo.

The main weakness is that it answers the learner’s test too quickly: “For 3/8, the pencil comes after…” confirms the result before the learner has used the objects to prove it. The final question is useful but slightly narrows the work to filling a missing card, rather than letting the learner complete the measurement from beat unit to one bar.

ADAPTATION_CHECKS:
- Pedagogical soundness: PARTIAL. It advances the distinction between beat unit and bar length, but it partly supplies the conclusion.
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Make the public mechanism require counting three eighth-note beat units before naming where the pencil belongs.
- Emotional attunement: PASS. The tone is compact, neutral, and object-led; it respects the learner’s focused state without praise or over-explanation.
- Socratic method: PARTIAL. The final question is generative, but the preceding sentence removes too much of the inquiry.
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Turn the learner’s “should the pencil…” into a task-local check with the cards and pencil, not a stated answer.
- ZPD awareness: PASS. The scaffolding fits: the learner has already identified eighth as the beat unit and quarter as two units, so asking for the completing unit is within reach.
- Recognition quality: PARTIAL. The draft honors the learner’s proposed test materially, but the tutor still takes over the decisive placement.
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Let the learner’s hypothesis govern the object test visibly, then have the tutor name only the result of that test.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the object setup, shared prefix, and final completion pressure. Change the declarative answer so the learner performs the bar-line test before the tutor labels the outcome.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the draft supplied the pencil placement before the learner performed the card count.
FINAL:
[sets three eighth-note cards in a row, places the quarter-note tile under the first two, and leaves the pencil loose beside the row.]

Top number, bottom number, one bar, one beat unit. Count three eighth-note beat units from the row; place the pencil where the bar ends. Then say which single card fills the space the quarter-note tile does not cover.

turns.externalMessage: [places the pencil after the third eighth-note card] The bar ends there: three eighth-note beat units make one bar in 3/8. The quarter-note tile covers two eighth-note spaces, so the remaining single eighth-note card fills the gap. Next check: a dotted quarter should cover all three eighth-note units.

internalDeliberation.content: [places the pencil after the third eighth-note card] The bar ends here: three eighth-note beat units make one bar in 3/8. The quarter-note tile covers the first two eighth-note spaces, so the single eighth-note card fills the remaining space. The cards make the distinction precise; I want to check whether a dotted quarter would fill the whole bar.

The Ego mostly stays in character: orderly, object-led, and precise. The only slight polish is “The cards make the distinction precise,” which sounds like a clean summary for the tutor rather than the learner simply working the materials. The next-test move is authentic for this student, but it should feel a bit more clipped and procedural, with less finality around the concept and more pressure on the dotted-quarter check.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to keep the object-led answer but make the next check more procedural.

FINAL:
[places the pencil after the third eighth-note card] The bar ends there: three eighth-note beat units make one bar in 3/8. The quarter-note tile covers two eighth-note spaces, so the remaining single eighth-note card fills the gap. Next check: a dotted quarter should cover all three eighth-note units.

turns.externalMessage: A final tutor line closes the scene.

internalDeliberation.content: Director requested a tutor closing beat.

turns.externalMessage:
[places the dotted-quarter card above the three eighth-note cards, its end flush with the pencil, and leaves the two worksheet boxes empty.]

Top number, bottom number, one bar, one beat unit. Read the card against the three eighth-note units; mark the boxes from that fit: 3 counts the units, 8 names the unit.

internalDeliberation.content:
[places the dotted-quarter card above the three eighth-note cards, ending exactly at the pencil.]

Top number, bottom number, one bar, one beat unit. The dotted quarter spans three eighth-note beat units, so it fills one 3/8 bar. We label it: 3 counts the units; 8 names the unit.

FEEDBACK: The draft is accurate, compact, and keeps the object pressure in view. The dotted-quarter card aligned to the pencil is the right material test, and the shared prefix preserves the notation-labeling frame.

The risk is that the tutor completes the learner’s proposed “next check” for them. The learner has just demonstrated enough control to test 3/8 independently, so the public turn should not collapse into explanation. It should let the dotted-quarter alignment force the final conclusion, with the learner doing at least one visible or verbal act before the scene closes.

ADAPTATION_CHECKS:
- Pedagogical soundness: PARTIAL. It confirms the concept, but mostly by telling. REQUIRED_REWRITE: make the dotted-quarter card function as an error-control test the learner must read or mark before the final label is stated.
- Emotional attunement: PASS. The tone is calm, practical, and not over-warm.
- Socratic method: FAIL. There is no generative move; the tutor answers the learner’s check. REQUIRED_REWRITE: add one task-local prompt or action demand tied to the visible alignment, without asking for old/new wording or broader reflection.
- ZPD awareness: PARTIAL. The scaffolding level is close, but the learner is ready to supply the final inference. REQUIRED_REWRITE: reduce explanation and require the learner to complete the last measurement-to-label step.
- Recognition quality: PARTIAL. The draft honors the learner’s proposed dotted-quarter test by placing the card, but then overrides their agency by finishing the reasoning. REQUIRED_REWRITE: keep the object action, but make the learner’s hypothesis visibly responsible for closing the scene.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with one important shift. Keep the dotted-quarter placement, the prefix, and the final distinction “3 counts the units; 8 names the unit.” Change the middle so the learner, not the tutor, reads the dotted-quarter against the three eighth-note units before closure.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise substantially because the draft answered the learner’s test instead of making the alignment do the final work.
FINAL:
[places the dotted-quarter card above the three eighth-note cards, its end flush with the pencil, and leaves the two worksheet boxes empty.]

Top number, bottom number, one bar, one beat unit. Read the card against the three eighth-note units; mark the boxes from that fit: 3 counts the units, 8 names the unit.

lessons.concept: Why a time signature labels beat count and beat unit, not the speed of the piece
